title: Shifting the Defense: Positioning for the Pull Hitter

root.narration: Top of the 6th, your team leads by one. Runner on first, nobody out. The next batter is a strong lefty who pulls EVERYTHING. Your scouting report shows that 78% of their ground balls go to the right side of the infield. Your coach calls out to the infield: 'How do we want to play this one?' This is your call, captain.

decision-1.narration: This lefty pulls almost everything to the right side. 78% of their grounders go right. How do you set up the defense?

choices.text: Shift the shortstop to the right side of second base, but keep the third baseman at third. Cover the pull AND protect against the bunt!
Full shift! Move everyone to the right side. Three infielders on the right, third baseman plays short right field.
Play straight up. I don't trust the numbers -- stay in normal positions.

transition-ball-hit-moderate.narration: Smart setup! Your shortstop moves to the right of second base, your second baseman shades toward first. The third baseman stays at third to prevent a bunt or a slap to the left side. You've got good coverage on the pull side AND you're not giving away the bunt. The pitcher delivers... the batter swings and RIPS a hard ground ball!

transition-ball-hit-full.narration: Full shift! You move the third baseman over to the right side of the infield. Now you've got three infielders on the right side of second base and the left side is WIDE OPEN. The batter notices the shift and starts thinking... The pitcher delivers and the batter chops a ground ball!

transition-ball-hit-normal.narration: You play everyone in their standard positions. The pitcher delivers and the batter does exactly what the scouting report said -- they pull a hard grounder to the right side!

decision-2-moderate.narration: The batter pulls a hard grounder right at your shifted shortstop, who's now playing on the right side of second base! The ball is right at them!

choices.text: Field it and flip to second for the force! The second baseman covers the bag!
Field it and throw to first! Get the sure out!
I'm right next to second base -- step on it myself for the force!

decision-2-full.narration: The batter sees the full shift and does something unexpected -- they push a BUNT down the THIRD base line! With no third baseman, the left side is completely empty! The ball is rolling slowly toward where the third baseman WOULD be!

choices.text: Pitcher has to charge over and field it! They're the closest!
Catcher sprints out from behind the plate to get it!
Nobody can get there. It's a hit. We gave that up by shifting fully.

decision-2-normal.narration: The grounder is pulled hard to the right side -- but it's in the gap between the first baseman and second baseman! In normal positioning, nobody can reach it!

choices.text: Second baseman dives to their right! Try to make a great play!
First baseman ranges to their left for the backhand!
It's through! Nobody can get it. Base hit to right field.

outcome-shift-dp-great.narration: Your shifted shortstop fields the ball cleanly right at their position! They flip to the second baseman covering the bag -- FORCE OUT! The second baseman turns and fires to first -- DOUBLE PLAY! The shift put your shortstop in the PERFECT position for this ball! What would have been a base hit through the normal 3-4 hole is now a double play! Your coach pumps their fist. Data wins!

outcome.headline: Double Play! The Shift Wins!
outcome.explanation: THIS is why we shift! The scouting report said 78% of this batter's grounders go to the right side, and that's exactly what happened. By moving your shortstop to the right side of second base, you turned what would have been a base hit into a double play. The moderate shift was the best choice because: 1) It covered the pull side with extra fielders. 2) It kept the third baseman at third to prevent bunts. 3) It allowed for a natural double play because the shortstop was close to second base. The analytics gave you the information, and your positioning used it perfectly.
outcome.whatToRemember: The moderate shift gives you the best of both worlds: extra coverage on the pull side AND protection against bunts on the open side. Trust the scouting data, but don't over-shift!

outcome-shift-first-okay.narration: You field it and throw to first. The batter is out, but the runner from first advances to second. One out, runner in scoring position. You got the out but didn't get the lead runner.

outcome.headline: Out at First, Runner Advances.
outcome.explanation: You got the out, which is good. But with the shortstop shifted right next to second base, you had a great chance to flip to second for the force on the lead runner AND turn a double play. By throwing to first, you chose the less valuable out and let the lead runner advance. When you're positioned near second base after the shift, think force-at-second first. The double play is available, and it's worth more than a simple putout at first.
outcome.whatToRemember: When the shift puts you near second base, use it! Flip to second for the force, then turn two. Don't default to throwing to first when a better play is right next to you.

outcome-shift-self-good.narration: You're right next to second base, so you step on the bag yourself for the force out on the lead runner. One out! But the batter reaches first safely because nobody was in position to take a throw there. Still, you got the more important out -- the lead runner.

outcome.headline: Force at Second! Lead Runner Erased!
outcome.explanation: Good instinct to get the lead runner! Stepping on second yourself got the force out on the more dangerous runner. But ideally, you'd flip to the second baseman covering the bag and then they'd relay to first for the double play. Doing it yourself means you can't throw to first in time because you've already committed your momentum to reaching the bag. In a shift, practice the flip to the covering fielder so you can still turn two. But between this and throwing to first, getting the lead runner is the smarter choice.
outcome.whatToRemember: Getting the lead runner at second is more valuable than the batter at first. But for the double play, flip to the covering fielder rather than tagging the base yourself -- it keeps the relay alive.

outcome-full-shift-bunt-bad.narration: The pitcher runs toward the third base line but the ball is rolling too slowly and too far from them. By the time they pick it up, the batter is already past first! The runner from first cruises into second. Everybody's safe -- runners on first and second, nobody out. The batter BEAT the full shift with a simple bunt!

outcome.headline: Bunt Base Hit! Shift Beaten!
outcome.explanation: This is the BIGGEST weakness of the full shift -- it gives up the entire left side of the infield! A smart batter will see no third baseman and just push a bunt or slap the ball down the third base line for a free hit. The full shift works great against batters who are stubborn and keep pulling the ball. But against smart hitters who can bunt or go the other way, the full shift gives them a gift. That's why the MODERATE shift (moving the shortstop but keeping the third baseman) is usually better -- it covers the pull side without giving up a free base on bunts.
outcome.whatToRemember: The full shift gives up the bunt to the open side. If the batter can bunt, use a moderate shift instead. Never give a smart hitter a free base!

outcome-full-shift-catcher-bad.narration: The catcher rips off their mask and sprints out from behind the plate... but it's way too far! They can't get to the ball in time. The bunt sits on the third base line, and by the time anyone gets there, everyone is safe. Runners on first and second, nobody out. And now the catcher has to hustle back behind the plate.

outcome.headline: Too Far! Catcher Can't Reach It!
outcome.explanation: The catcher starts behind home plate, which is nearly 90 feet from where the bunt stopped on the third base line. That's way too far to field and make a throw. Also, sending the catcher out leaves home plate uncovered, which is dangerous if there's a play there. The real problem isn't who fields the bunt -- it's that the full shift left the entire left side open. When you shift that dramatically, you're INVITING the bunt. If the batter takes you up on it, there's no good answer. That's why the moderate shift (keeping the third baseman at third) is the smarter approach against any batter who can bunt.
outcome.whatToRemember: The catcher should stay behind the plate. The full shift's weakness (open left side) can't be solved by moving the catcher -- solve it by not fully shifting. Use the moderate shift instead.

outcome-full-shift-accept-okay.narration: You accept it -- the bunt is a hit and nobody can reach it. The runner moves to second, the batter reaches first. Runners on first and second, nobody out. You acknowledge that the full shift was beaten. The batter adjusted, and you have to adjust back next time.

outcome.headline: Hit. Adjust for Next Time.
outcome.explanation: Smart to recognize that you can't panic and make a wild play. The full shift was beaten by a bunt -- that's a known weakness. The lesson here is for NEXT time: if this batter shows they'll bunt against the full shift, you need to adjust. Use the moderate shift (shortstop to the right side but keep third baseman) or even play straight up. Baseball is a constant chess match of adjustments. The analytics said to shift, but analytics don't account for a batter who's willing to change their approach. The BEST defenders combine data with game-time awareness.
outcome.whatToRemember: When a batter beats your shift with a bunt, ADJUST next time. Use a moderate shift or play straight up. Defense is a chess match -- always adapt to what the batter is showing you.

outcome-no-shift-dive-okay.narration: The second baseman dives to their right... stretches out... and just barely knocks it down! But they can't make a throw from the ground. The batter reaches first, and the runner advances to second. No outs. The ball went right to where your shifted shortstop WOULD HAVE BEEN standing.

outcome.headline: Knocked Down But No Out. Shift Would Have Had It!
outcome.explanation: That ball went RIGHT to where a shifted shortstop would have been standing! Instead, your second baseman had to make a diving play and couldn't complete the throw. If you had used the moderate shift, the shortstop would have been standing right there -- no dive needed -- and could have started a double play. The scouting report was RIGHT: the batter pulled the ball to the right side. By ignoring the data and playing straight up, you turned what should have been an easy play into a diving knockdown with no out. Trust the analytics!
outcome.whatToRemember: When the scouting report says a batter pulls 78% of the time, BELIEVE IT. The shift puts your fielders where the ball is most likely to go. Playing straight up ignores valuable information.

outcome-no-shift-first-okay.narration: The first baseman ranges to their left and gets a glove on it... but the ball skips off the edge of their glove and into right field! Base hit! The runner from first races to third on the error, and the batter is on first. Runners on first and third, nobody out. That ball was in the gap where a shifted fielder would have been.

outcome.headline: Error! Ball Gets Through!
outcome.explanation: The first baseman made a valiant effort, but that ball was hit to the pull side -- exactly where the scouting report said it would go. With a shift, either the second baseman or the shifted shortstop would have been standing right there, no stretching needed. Instead, you asked the first baseman to make a highlight-reel play just to keep it to a single, and the ball got through. The numbers were clear: 78% of ground balls go to the right side. Ignoring that information cost you a potential double play and gave the other team runners on the corners.
outcome.whatToRemember: Positioning is about putting fielders where the ball is LIKELY to go, not where tradition says they should stand. Trust the data and shift accordingly!

outcome-no-shift-hit-bad.narration: The ball goes right through the gap between first and second -- clean single to right field! The runner from first rounds second and heads to third. Nobody could even get close to the ball. Your shortstop, standing on the LEFT side of second base, watches the ball sail past them 15 feet to their right. A shift would have had them standing right there.

outcome.headline: Base Hit Through the Hole! Shift Would Have Caught It!
outcome.explanation: The scouting report was 100% right. The batter pulled the ball to the right side, exactly where 78% of their ground balls go. Your fielders were in their traditional positions, which means the shortstop was on the WRONG side of the field. If you had shifted even moderately, the shortstop would have been standing right where that ball went. You had the information to prevent this hit and you chose to ignore it. In modern baseball and softball, positioning is based on DATA, not just tradition. The shift exists because it works -- when used wisely.
outcome.whatToRemember: When you have scouting data showing where a batter hits the ball, USE IT! Traditional positioning is a starting point, but smart teams adjust based on each batter's tendencies.
